title: Abaca fibre sheet forming
subtitle: Forming translucent, long-fibre sheets from Manila hemp pulp
excerpt: Abaca (Manila hemp) produces one of the longest natural fibres used in hand papermaking. The resulting sheets are translucent, very strong for their weight, and ideal for lamp shades, book tissue, and layered paper art. The forming method is standard Western sheet forming, but the long fibres need specific beating and dilution to form well.
sourceNotes: Timothy Barrett, Paper Through Time: Nondestructive Analysis of 14th- through 19th-Century Papers (2013), University of Iowa. Barrett's comparative fibre studies document abaca's long-fibre behaviour. Internet Archive. Dard Hunter, Papermaking: The History and Technique of an Ancient Craft (1947), chapter on fibre preparation.

content.text: fibre makes sheets that are thinner and more translucent than cotton linter at the same weight. Abaca fibres are long and bond at fewer crossings per square centimetre. The method is Western sheet forming (vat, mould and deckle, couch, press). Abaca's long fibres need a dilute vat and an unhurried pull to achieve good
. This tutorial uses pre-processed abaca half-stuff, sold wet or dry, with no cooking or retting needed.

items.qty: 30 g dry weight for a small vat
items.name: Abaca half-stuff (pre-processed fibre, sold by specialist papermaking suppliers)
Vat (large enough to immerse the mould fully, with 10 cm clearance on each side)
Hollander beater or kitchen blender (for preparing the pulp)

content.text: Soak dry abaca half-stuff in cold water for at least 4 hours (overnight is better). The fibres will absorb water and soften but will not dissolve.

Beat or blend in small batches. In a kitchen blender: fill to two-thirds with water, add a small pinch of soaked fibre (no more than 2 g dry weight per blend cycle), pulse 10 to 15 times. Over-blending shortens the fibres and reduces the characteristic translucency. The target is a loose, slightly fluffy slurry with no unopened fibre clumps.

Add the blended pulp to the vat and dilute with water until the vat holds at least 20 litres. Abaca forms best at a lower concentration than cotton linter: if the vat looks milky rather than cloudy, the concentration is about right. Stir thoroughly before each sheet.

Stir the vat to distribute the fibres evenly, then immediately lower the mould and deckle to the far edge of the vat at a 45-degree angle. In a single continuous movement, bring the mould to horizontal under the surface and draw it toward you, lifting straight up as it clears the near edge of the vat. Keep the lift slow and level.
Hold the mould level while the water drains. Abaca drains faster than cotton due to its higher
. Tilt slightly front and back (not side to side) once during drainage to even the formation. Do not shake; abaca fibres align quickly and shaking produces wavy texture marks.

Once free water has stopped dripping, blot the underside of the mould with the sponge and couch onto a couching cloth using the rolling motion described in the pressing-and-drying tutorial. Press and dry by any method. Abaca sheets can be board-dried for a very smooth, translucent surface.

A thin abaca sheet (30 to 40 gsm) glued over a plain sheet adds a textured, translucent layer for bookbinding endpapers. Sheets formed at lower concentration (around 20 gsm) are translucent enough for lamp shades and window panels when dry. Abaca is also used as a tissue layer in paper conservation work.

attrs.intro: Problems specific to abaca's long-fibre character.

items.fix: Blend each batch a little longer. Stir thoroughly, pull within 5 seconds of stirring.
items.cause: Pulp not sufficiently beaten, or vat not stirred immediately before pulling.
items.symptom: Sheet has heavy clumps and thin patches (poor formation)

items.fix: Dilute the vat further. Allow at least 30 minutes under weight before attempting to separate the sheet.
items.cause: Sheet pulled too thick for the mould size, or insufficient pressing before lifting.
items.symptom: Sheet tears when lifted from the couching cloth

attrs.heading: Common problems with abaca
